solution: To invert, set \( [[a]]x + ([[b]]) = [[y0]] \) and solve for \( x \): \( x = \dfrac{[[y0]] - ([[b]])}{[[a]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This applies \( f \) itself to \( [[y0]] \) instead of inverting \( f \).
This subtracts \( b \) but forgets to divide by \( a \).
This negates the correct value (a sign slip when isolating \( x \)).

solution: Set \( \dfrac{[[a]]x + ([[b]])}{[[c]]x + ([[d]])} = [[y0]] \), cross-multiply to get \( [[a]]x + ([[b]]) = [[y0]]\big([[c]]x + ([[d]])\big) \), and collect the \( x \) terms to solve: \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This just restates the given output value \( [[y0]] \) instead of solving for \( x \).
This negates the correct value (a sign slip when isolating \( x \)).
This cross-multiplies but uses the wrong side's coefficients when isolating \( x \).
This swaps which coefficients multiply \( y_0 \) versus which stand alone, a common bookkeeping slip.
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: Since \( h(x) = [[a]]([[c]]x + ([[d]])) + ([[b]]) \), setting \( h(x) = [[y0]] \) and solving for \( x \) (equivalently, applying \( g^{-1} \) then \( f^{-1} \) in reverse order) gives \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This applies only \( f^{-1} \) to \( [[y0]] \), stopping before undoing \( g \) as well.
This applies only \( g^{-1} \) to \( [[y0]] \), forgetting that \( f \) must be undone FIRST since it was applied last.
This negates the correct value (a sign slip when isolating \( x \)).

solution: Set \( [[a]]^{x} + ([[b]]) = [[y0]] \), isolate the exponential as \( [[a]]^{x} = [[y0]] - ([[b]]) \), then take \( \log_{[[a]]} \) of both sides: \( x = \log_{[[a]]}\!\big([[y0]] - ([[b]])\big) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to take the logarithm, leaving the isolated power itself.
This forgets to subtract \( [[b]] \) before taking the logarithm.
This divides by \( [[a]] \) instead of taking a logarithm base \( [[a]] \).
This negates the correct exponent (a sign slip).
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: Set \( [[a]]\sqrt{x-([[h]])} + ([[k]]) = [[y0]] \), isolate the radical as \( \sqrt{x-([[h]])} = \dfrac{[[y0]] - ([[k]])}{[[a]]} \), then square both sides: \( x = [[h]] + \left(\dfrac{[[y0]]-([[k]])}{[[a]]}\right)^{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This isolates the radical correctly but forgets to square it before adding to \( [[h]] \).
This subtracts the squared term instead of adding it.
This squares the isolated radical correctly but forgets to add \( [[h]] \).
This reports the domain's left endpoint \( [[h]] \) rather than solving for the actual input.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = x^{5} + [[a]]x^{3} + [[b]]x \), which is one-to-one on the real numbers. Given that \( f([[x0]]) = [[V]] \), what is \( f^{-1}([[V]]) \)?
solution: By the defining property of an inverse function, \( f^{-1}(f(a)) = a \) for any \( a \) where \( f \) is one-to-one. Since \( f([[x0]]) = [[V]] \), it follows immediately that \( f^{-1}([[V]]) = [[x0]] = [[answer]] \) — no algebraic inversion of the quintic is needed.
distractor_rationales: This reports the OUTPUT value \( [[V]] \) itself, confusing it with the input that \( f^{-1} \) should recover.
This negates the correct input value.
This subtracts the input from the output for no valid reason.
This reports the reciprocal of the input instead of the input itself.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
